---
title: "Sampling Distribution"
description: "The probability distribution of a statistic (such as the sample mean) computed from many repeated samples drawn from the same data-generating process."
---

In probability, we usually start by studying the distribution of a **random variable**.

For example, when rolling a fair die, we may ask:

> What is the probability that the outcome is 4?

Here the object of interest is the **distribution of the random variable** that represents the outcome of the die.

In statistical inference, however, the question changes.

Instead of studying the distribution of a single observation, we study **statistics computed from samples**.

A useful way to think about this shift is the following:

> When you roll one die, the natural question is about the distribution of the random variable.  
> When you roll several dice and compute a statistic, the question becomes the distribution of that statistic.

For example, suppose we roll a die **four times** and compute the **sample mean** of the outcomes.

Each set of four rolls produces a different value for the sample mean.

If we repeated this experiment many times - each time rolling four dice and computing the mean - we would obtain many possible values of that statistic.

The distribution of those possible values is called the **sampling distribution**.

Formally, a **sampling distribution** is the probability distribution of a statistic computed from a random sample drawn from a population or from a [Data Generating Process (DGP)](dgp.qmd).

Sampling distributions play a central role in statistical inference because they allow us to understand how statistics behave across repeated samples.

Many of the most important results in statistics - such as confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and the **[Central Limit Theorem](clt.qmd)** - rely on properties of sampling distributions.

## Connection to the Central Limit Theorem

In many practical situations, deriving the exact sampling distribution of a statistic can be difficult.

A fundamental result in statistics helps us in this situation: the **[Central Limit Theorem](clt.qmd)**.

The Central Limit Theorem states that, under fairly general conditions, the sampling distribution of the **sample mean** becomes approximately **normal (Gaussian)** as the sample size increases, regardless of the shape of the original distribution.

In other words, even if the underlying data-generating process is not normally distributed, the distribution of the sample mean tends to become approximately normal when the sample size is sufficiently large.

This result explains why the normal distribution appears so frequently in statistical inference and why it is widely used for constructing confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

A more detailed discussion of the Central Limit Theorem is provided in the dedicated section on the **[Central Limit Theorem](clt.qmd)**.

## Where Sampling Distributions Appear in Data Science and Machine Learning

Although the concept originates in classical statistics, sampling distributions appear frequently in modern data science and machine learning.

Some common examples include:

- **Model evaluation**  
  Metrics such as accuracy, AUC, precision, or recall are statistics computed from a test dataset.  
  Their variability across different samples reflects the **sampling distribution of the evaluation metric**.

- **Cross-validation**  
  When a model is trained and evaluated across multiple folds, the resulting scores approximate the **sampling distribution of the model's performance**.

- **Bootstrap methods**  
  Bootstrapping repeatedly resamples the dataset and recomputes a statistic (such as a coefficient or prediction error) to approximate its **sampling distribution**.

- **Uncertainty quantification**  
  Confidence intervals and standard errors used in regression or causal inference rely on theoretical results about sampling distributions.

Even in predictive machine learning pipelines, whenever we compute statistics from data - such as model parameters, evaluation metrics, or feature importance measures - their variability across samples is governed by **sampling distributions**.
